A mat of duckweed parts without a ripple as a long shape glides closer. The crocodile waits until the bank crumbles under someone's boot.
The sunset brings a whining cloud out from the cypress knees. Stirges cling to trunks, reeds, and packs until warm blood draws them in.
Smoke curls from a reed camp hidden behind hanging moss. The lizardfolk there move to protect their fishing grounds the moment strangers step too close.
Dry-looking hummocks form a broken path through black water. Testing each one reveals which are rooted and which float over deep mud.
Bubbles pop around a rotten stump just before swamp gas catches a stray spark. A fast dive into the mud avoids the brief green flame.
Cool water gathers under cypress shade where the mud smells clean instead of foul. Washing there eases insect bites and swamp rash.
Red cranberries float among glossy leaves on a wet mat of moss. Harvesting them means kneeling where the ground slowly sinks.
Cattails grow thick in a shallow channel. Their roots are filling, but pulling them clouds the water and hides leeches.
Blue flowers bloom on a hummock circled by dark water. The leaves are medicinal if clipped before the petals close.
Catfish stir in a muddy hole under a fallen log. Reaching them means working by feel while something bumps the log from below.
A narrow ditch snaps and clicks with crawdads under leaf litter. A baited basket fills quickly if placed before the water clouds.
Long gar hover in black water, their scales flashing when they turn. Hooking one requires patience and a line stronger than it looks.
Rust-colored water seeps around a peat shelf, leaving iron nodules beneath the mud. Each one must be felt out by hand.
A fallen bank exposes sticky blue clay below the peat. It is clean enough to gather if kept clear of roots and swamp water.
A dry rise holds peat dense enough to cut into fuel bricks. The trick is taking straight blocks before water fills the cut.
A dead cypress has fallen across two hummocks, its heartwood still sound above the waterline. Cutting it free shifts the balance of both banks.
Old floodwater left drift logs tangled in hanging moss. The dry upper halves can be salvaged if the lower rot is avoided.
Willows lean over the blackwater in tight clusters. Their young shoots make flexible rods if cut before they split.
Reeds rustle in a perfect circle around a muddy pool. The croaking stops all at once, and spears rise from the water.
A blue light drifts above black water and retreats whenever followed. The corpse beneath it still clutches a lantern.
A bank of rotting reeds heaves up in one great wet mass. Lightning-scarred roots writhe through it like veins.
Bone-white stakes mark a safe path through pools that all look alike. The order changes after floods, and only fresh moss shows the truth.
A bubble of marsh gas bursts near a lantern and rolls blue fire across the water. Diving flat keeps the flare overhead.
A ring of cypress knees shelters dry moss above the waterline. Resting there keeps insects away just long enough to breathe.
Marsh mallow grows where the water turns shallow and sweet. The roots must be washed quickly before mud dries into them.
Cranberries gleam on a floating mat that shifts underfoot. Steady balance keeps the harvest from sinking into black water.
Silver moss hangs from cypress branches and dries into useful padding. The cleanest strands grow above leech height.
Mudfish hide in burrows along a bank of wet clay. They can be coaxed out with bait before the hole collapses.
Eels writhe among leeches in a shallow pool. A basket works if lifted fast and kept away from bare skin.
Crawdads crowd the roots of a half-drowned tree. Reaching them means feeling through mud for snapping claws.
Amber beads cling to an old cypress root exposed by floodwater. Each bead needs gentle work to free.
A skiff lies half-sunk in duckweed. Its boards are stained but still useful after the mud is scraped away.
Straight mangrove poles grow in brackish shallows. Cutting mature poles opens channels for young growth.
Smooth greenstone peeks through black mud at the base of a drowned standing stone. Every useful piece must be washed before it vanishes again.
A midden of old shells rises above the reeds, dry enough to burn into lime. Sorting clean shell from refuse takes a patient eye.
A bog oak root lies exposed by receding water, dark and dense from years under peat. It is heavy, valuable, and stubbornly wedged.
